learning.summary: An in-place upgrade installs a newer version of an operating system while preserving existing applications, user data, and most settings. It is commonly used when upgrading to a supported newer release.
learning.explanation:
An in-place upgrade replaces the existing operating system with a newer version without erasing the user's files or installed applications. This minimizes disruption compared to reinstalling the operating system from scratch.

Before performing an in-place upgrade, compatibility should be verified to ensure the hardware, applications, and current operating system support the upgrade path. Unlike a clean install, an in-place upgrade is designed to preserve user data and installed software.

facts.text: An in-place upgrade preserves applications and user data.
An in-place upgrade installs a newer version of the operating system.
Compatibility should be verified before performing an in-place upgrade.

examples.text: Upgrade Windows 10 to Windows 11 while keeping installed applications and user documents.

examTips: Know the difference between an in-place upgrade and a clean install.
commonMistakes: Assuming every operating system version supports an in-place upgrade.
scenarios: Recommend an in-place upgrade when the user wants to keep applications and data.
pbqIdeas: Choose the appropriate installation method based on user requirements.

reviewNotes: Compatibility guidance is curriculum enrichment beyond the explicit transcript evidence.
